title: HTML basics
short_description: How web pages are structured with HTML elements, tags, and attributes that QA engineers target when locating UI controls.
learning_goals:
Read raw HTML and identify the element behind any control on a page.
Name the common tags (div, span, a, button, input, form, table) and explain what each renders.
Locate and use the attributes QA relies on for selectors: id, class, name, type, value, data-* and ARIA attributes.
Explain the difference between an element's tag, its attributes, its text content, and its child elements.
theory:
HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the skeleton of every web page. The browser receives an HTML document as plain text, parses it, and builds a tree of elements that it then renders on screen. As a QA automation engineer you do not write much HTML, but you read it constantly: every locator you write (a CSS selector or XPath) points at an element in that HTML tree, so you must be able to look at the markup and predict what the user sees. An element is written with tags. A typical element looks like Log in. Here is the opening tag, is the closing tag, 'a' is the tag name (an anchor/link), href and class and id are attributes (name="value" pairs), and 'Log in' is the text content. Some elements are self-closing and have no text or children, like or . A handful of attributes matter enormously for testing. The id is meant to be unique on the page, so #login-link is the most stable selector you can get. The class attribute can hold several space-separated values (class="btn primary") and is shared by many elements, so it groups styling. For forms, the name and type attributes identify inputs. Modern teams add custom attributes like data-testid="submit-order" specifically so automation has a stable hook that does not break when designers rename CSS classes. Elements nest to form a hierarchy: a
contains and
key_terms: element, tag, attribute, id, class, text content, self-closing tag, data-testid, semantic HTML, ARIA
real_world_example: You are automating a checkout flow and the 'Place order' button keeps breaking your test after every release. You open the HTML and see <button class="btn btn--lg sc-1a2b3c">Place order</button> — the class names are generated by the build tool and change on each deploy. You ask the developers to add data-testid="place-order" to the button. Now your locator [data-testid='place-order'] stays stable no matter how the styling changes, and the flaky failures stop.
step_by_step:
1. Open any web page and press F12 to open DevTools, then go to the Elements panel.
2. Click the inspect (arrow) icon and click a control on the page, for example a login button.
3. Read the highlighted line: note the tag name, the id, the class list, and any data-* attributes.
4. Look at the indentation above and below the element to see its parent and sibling elements.
5. Right-click the element and choose 'Edit as HTML' to view the exact raw markup including text content.
6. Identify which single attribute would uniquely and stably target this element (prefer id or data-testid).
7. Repeat for an <input> field and note its name and type attributes, which forms and validation rely on.
common_mistakes: Assuming class is unique like id — class is almost always shared by many elements, so relying on one class often matches the wrong node. Confusing the visible text with an attribute value; 'Log in' text and id="login-link" are different things and need different locators. Forgetting that self-closing tags like and have no text content, so trying to match their inner text always fails. Ignoring whitespace and nesting — copying a chunk of HTML out of context and not realizing the target element lives several levels deep. Treating auto-generated class names (like sc-1a2b3c or css-9f8e7d) as stable; they change on every build.
practical_use_case: Every time you write or debug an automated UI test you read HTML to choose a locator, confirm an element exists, or understand why a test cannot find a control. During exploratory and bug-reporting work you inspect HTML to capture the exact element involved, check accessibility attributes, and give developers precise reproduction details.
summary: HTML is the tree of elements that makes up a page; each element has a tag, attributes, text, and a position in the hierarchy. QA reads HTML to choose stable locators, preferring id and data-testid over volatile class names, and to understand the parent-child structure that selectors navigate.
example:
```html
<form id="login-form" action="/login" method="post">
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>

  <label for="password">Password</label>
  <input type="password" id="password" name="password" required>

  <button type="submit" data-testid="login-submit" class="btn btn--primary">
    Log in
  </button>
</form>
```
In this snippet the form is uniquely identified by id="login-form", the email field by id="email" (or name="email"), and the submit button by the stable data-testid="login-submit". The class "btn btn--primary" is for styling and should not be used as the primary locator.
interactive: Given this HTML, name (a) the tag, (b) the most stable attribute to use as a locator, and (c) the text content of the element: My Cart (3)
expected_answer: (a) The tag is 'a' — an anchor/link element. (b) The most stable locators are id="cart-link" or data-testid="cart"; data-testid is intended specifically for automation, and id is unique on the page. The class "nav-item cart" is shared styling and is less reliable. (c) The text content is 'My Cart (3)'. Note that href="/cart" is the link target, not the text or a primary locator.
homework: Pick any public website (for example an online store or a news site). Open DevTools and inspect five different controls: a navigation link, a search input, a primary button, a form field, and one item in a list. For each, write down the tag name, all attributes, the text content, and which single attribute you would use as a locator and why.
expected_result: A short document or table with five rows. Each row should correctly identify the tag, list the real attributes (id, class, name, type, data-* as present), capture the actual text content, and justify the chosen locator — ideally favoring id or data-testid over class, and explaining that class names may change between releases.

quiz.question: In <button id="save" class="btn primary">Save</button>, which part is the text content?
Which attribute is intended to be unique on a single HTML page?
Why do teams add data-testid attributes to elements?
answers: To provide a stable hook for automation that survives CSS changes
Because the browser requires it to render forms
To replace the need for HTML tags

quiz.question: Which of these is a self-closing element with no text content?

title: CSS basics
short_description: How CSS styles HTML, and how its selectors, classes, and the cascade affect what QA engineers see and target on a page.
learning_goals:
Explain how CSS rules (selector + declaration block) style HTML elements.
Distinguish a CSS class used for styling from one safe to use as a test locator.
Understand the cascade, specificity, and inheritance well enough to reason about why an element looks a certain way.
Identify CSS-driven states (hover, focus, disabled, display:none, visibility:hidden) that change whether a control is visible or interactable in a test.
theory:
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) controls how HTML looks: colors, spacing, layout, fonts, and which elements are visible. A CSS rule has two parts — a selector that picks elements, and a declaration block of property: value pairs. For example .btn--primary { background: #2563eb; color: #fff; padding: 8px 16px; } selects every element with the class btn--primary and paints it blue with white text. QA must understand CSS because the same class-based selectors used for styling are also where automation often hooks in, and because CSS decides whether an element is actually visible and clickable.

Selectors are the bridge between CSS and test automation. A tag selector (button) matches all buttons, a class selector (.btn) matches by class, and an id selector (#login) matches by id. CSS selectors are case-sensitive for class and id names and are the exact same syntax you later use in Selenium or Playwright with By.cssSelector. So learning CSS selectors here pays off directly when you write tests.

The 'cascading' part means many rules can target the same element, and the browser resolves conflicts using specificity and source order. Specificity is roughly: inline styles beat ids, ids beat classes, classes beat tag selectors. So #save { color: red } wins over .btn { color: blue } on the same element. As QA this explains visual bugs: a button looks wrong because a higher-specificity rule overrode the intended style. It also warns you that class-based locators can be brittle when build tools generate hashed names like css-1q2w3e.

CSS controls visibility in ways that directly affect tests. display:none removes the element from layout entirely — automation frameworks treat it as not present/not visible and a click will fail. visibility:hidden keeps the space but hides the element, and opacity:0 makes it invisible but still clickable. A very common flaky-test cause is trying to interact with an element that is technically in the DOM but hidden by CSS, or one that is covered by another element with a higher z-index.

CSS also defines interactive states using pseudo-classes: :hover, :focus, :active, :disabled, and :checked. A disabled button rendered with :disabled styling cannot be clicked, and a tooltip that only appears on :hover may need the test to hover first. Understanding these states helps you write tests that wait for the right condition (for example, wait until a button is enabled) instead of failing intermittently.
key_terms: selector, declaration block, class, specificity, cascade, inheritance, pseudo-class, display:none, visibility:hidden, z-index
real_world_example: A test that clicks the 'Submit' button fails with an 'element not interactable' error, but the button is clearly visible to you. You open DevTools, select the button, and in the Styles panel you see that a loading overlay with z-index:1000 and position:fixed is sitting on top of it. The button is in the DOM and visible, but the overlay intercepts the click. The fix is to wait for the overlay (display becomes none) to disappear before clicking — a CSS-state problem, not a locator problem.
step_by_step:
1. Open DevTools and select an element in the Elements panel.
2. Open the Styles panel on the right to see every CSS rule applied to it, top rule winning.
3. Note crossed-out properties — those lost the specificity battle and were overridden.
4. Check the Computed tab to see the final resolved value of any property like display or color.
5. Look for display, visibility, and opacity to confirm whether the element is truly visible.
6. Toggle a pseudo-class state (the :hov button) to preview :hover/:focus/:disabled styling.
7. Inspect overlapping elements and their z-index if a click is being intercepted.
common_mistakes:
Assuming an element you can see is clickable, when display:none, an overlay, or :disabled blocks interaction.
Using a hashed/generated class (css-1q2w3e, sc-AbCdEf) as a locator; it changes on every build and breaks tests.
Not understanding specificity, so you can't explain why a style 'isn't applying' when a more specific rule overrides it.
Forgetting that visibility:hidden and opacity:0 still occupy space and may still be in the accessibility tree, unlike display:none.
Writing locators based on color or computed style rather than structural attributes — styling is not a reliable identifier.
practical_use_case: QA uses CSS knowledge daily to write CSS-selector locators, to debug 'element not visible/not interactable' failures, to verify visual states (a button correctly disabled until a form is valid), and to file precise visual bugs that point developers at the exact overriding rule or hidden element.
summary: CSS pairs selectors with style declarations and resolves conflicts through specificity and the cascade. For QA, CSS matters because its selectors are reused as test locators and because properties like display, visibility, opacity, and z-index decide whether an element is actually visible and clickable.
interactive: An element has both .btn { color: blue } and #save { color: red } applied, plus the inline style style="color: green". What color does the text render, and why?
expected_answer: The text renders green. Specificity order from lowest to highest is: tag selectors, then classes (.btn), then ids (#save), then inline styles. The inline style="color: green" has the highest specificity of these three, so it wins over both the id rule (red) and the class rule (blue). If the inline style were removed, #save (red) would win over .btn (blue) because an id beats a class.
homework: On a page with a form, find a button that is disabled until the form is filled in. Using DevTools, document: (1) the CSS rule(s) that apply the disabled styling, (2) the difference in computed 'display'/'opacity' between disabled and enabled states, and (3) one element on the page hidden with display:none and one hidden with visibility:hidden, noting how each behaves differently in layout.
expected_result: A write-up showing the actual :disabled rule (with property values like opacity), a before/after comparison of the relevant computed properties, and two correctly classified hidden elements — explaining that display:none removes the element from layout and from interaction, while visibility:hidden keeps its space but hides it.

quiz.question: Which CSS property setting makes an element disappear AND removes it from page layout, so an automated click fails because it's treated as not present?
In the cascade, which of these wins when targeting the same element?
Why is a class like 'css-1q2w3e' a poor choice for a test locator?
answers: Classes can never be used in selectors
It is auto-generated and typically changes on each build

quiz.question: A button is visible but a click throws 'element not interactable'. Which CSS cause is most likely?
answers: The button text is too long
Another element with a higher z-index overlays it

title: DOM basics
short_description: What the DOM is, how it differs from raw HTML, and how QA navigates its tree to find, inspect, and interact with elements.
learning_goals:
Define the DOM as the live, in-memory tree the browser builds from HTML and updates with JavaScript.
Explain why the live DOM can differ from the HTML returned by the server (View Source vs Elements).
Navigate the DOM tree using parent, child, and sibling relationships.
Understand how dynamic DOM changes (added/removed nodes, single-page apps) cause stale-element and timing issues in tests.
theory:
The DOM (Document Object Model) is the browser's live, in-memory representation of the page as a tree of nodes. When the browser loads HTML it parses the text once into this tree; from then on, the page you interact with IS the DOM, not the original HTML text. JavaScript can add, remove, or change nodes at any time, so the DOM is dynamic and constantly changing while the static HTML file never does. This distinction is crucial for QA. 'View Source' (Ctrl+U) shows the original HTML the server sent. The DevTools Elements panel shows the current DOM after JavaScript has run. On a modern single-page app (React, Vue, Angular) View Source may be nearly empty — just a
— while the Elements panel shows a full page. Your automation tools operate on the live DOM, so always inspect via Elements, not View Source, when building locators. The DOM is a tree of nodes with relationships: every node has one parent, may have children, and has siblings (nodes sharing the same parent). The document is the root, is its child, then and , and so on down to text nodes. These relationships are exactly what XPath axes (parent, child, following-sibling) and CSS combinators (>, +, ~) walk. Knowing that a price is the next sibling of a product-name lets you locate it relative to a known element. Because the DOM changes at runtime, two classic automation problems arise. First, timing: if your test looks for an element before JavaScript has added it, the locator finds nothing — the fix is an explicit wait for the element to appear. Second, stale element references: you grab a reference to a node, then the framework re-renders and replaces that node with a new one; your old reference now points at a node no longer in the tree, and the framework throws a StaleElementReferenceException. The remedy is to re-locate the element right before using it. QA also uses the DOM programmatically. In the DevTools Console you can run document.querySelector('#login') to test a locator before putting it in a script, or count matches with document.querySelectorAll('.product').length. This is the fastest way to validate that a selector is unique and correct without running the whole test suite.
key_terms: DOM, node, document root, parent/child/sibling, render, single-page application, stale element reference, querySelector, dynamic DOM, View Source
real_world_example: You write a test for a SPA that opens a modal and clicks 'Confirm'. It passes locally but fails in CI with 'no such element'. Inspecting the live DOM you confirm the modal is injected by JavaScript a few hundred milliseconds after the button click, so in the faster CI environment the test looks for 'Confirm' before the modal node exists. You replace the immediate find with an explicit wait for the modal's [data-testid='confirm'] to be present and visible, and the test becomes reliable.
step_by_step:
1. On any page press Ctrl+U (View Source) and note how much HTML the server sent.
2. Open DevTools Elements and compare — on a SPA the live DOM has far more nodes.
3. Select an element and use the breadcrumb trail at the bottom to walk up its parents.
4. Use the Console: run document.querySelectorAll('.product').length to count matching nodes.
5. Run document.querySelector('#main .price') to confirm a relative locator returns the right node.
6. Trigger a dynamic change (open a menu/modal) and watch new nodes appear in the Elements tree.
7. Note which nodes get removed/replaced on re-render — these are stale-reference risks in tests.
common_mistakes:
Building locators from View Source instead of the live DOM, so selectors that exist after JavaScript runs appear 'missing'.
Not waiting for dynamic content, causing 'no such element' failures when the node hasn't been added yet.
Reusing an element reference after the page re-rendered, triggering a stale element reference error.
Assuming the DOM is static and unchanging, when SPAs constantly add and remove nodes.
Confusing a node being in the DOM with it being visible/interactable — presence and visibility are different conditions.
practical_use_case: QA navigates and queries the DOM every time they validate a locator in the Console, debug why an element can't be found, decide where to add an explicit wait, or trace a relationship (find the delete button inside the row whose name cell matches a value). It is also central to debugging flaky SPA tests.
summary: The DOM is the browser's live tree of nodes, built from HTML and continuously modified by JavaScript, which is why it can differ from View Source. QA navigates parent/child/sibling relationships to locate elements and must account for the DOM's dynamic nature with explicit waits and re-locating to avoid timing and stale-element failures.
interactive: In the DevTools Console of any page, write one line that returns the number of <a> link elements currently in the DOM. Then explain why this count might differ from the number of <a> tags you see in View Source.
expected_answer: The line is document.querySelectorAll('a').length (or document.getElementsByTagName('a').length). It returns a number, for example 47. The count can differ from View Source because View Source shows only the original server HTML, while the Console queries the live DOM after JavaScript has run — scripts may have injected additional links (menus, lazy-loaded content, SPA-rendered navigation) or removed some, so the live count is the accurate one for automation.
homework: Choose a single-page application (for example a web email client, a social feed, or a modern e-commerce site). Compare View Source with the live DOM and document: (1) roughly how many nodes the server HTML contains versus the live DOM, (2) one element that only exists after JavaScript runs, and (3) one user action that adds new nodes to the DOM, with a note on how you would wait for those nodes in a test.
expected_result: A short report contrasting the sparse server HTML (often a near-empty root div) with a much richer live DOM, naming a specific JS-injected element with its locator, and describing a real interaction (open menu, load more, open modal) that adds nodes — plus a concrete waiting strategy such as an explicit wait for the new node to be present and visible.

quiz.question: What does the DevTools Elements panel show that 'View Source' does not?
answers: The live DOM after JavaScript has modified the page

quiz.question: A StaleElementReferenceException usually happens because:
answers: The page has too much CSS
The element was re-rendered/replaced after you grabbed its reference
The element id is too long

quiz.question: Which Console call counts how many '.product' elements are in the live DOM right now?
On a React single-page app, why might 'View Source' look almost empty?
answers: The real content is rendered into the DOM by JavaScript after load
The browser blocks View Source for SPAs
The server sends CSS instead of HTML

title: Browser DevTools
short_description: Using the browser's built-in DevTools panels (Elements, Console, Network, Application) to inspect pages, validate locators, and investigate bugs.
learning_goals:
Open and navigate the core DevTools panels: Elements, Console, Network, and Application.
Inspect any element, read its HTML and applied CSS, and copy a working selector.
Use the Console to test CSS/JS locators and read JavaScript errors.
Use the Network panel to inspect HTTP requests, status codes (200, 404, 500), payloads, and timing for bug investigation.
theory:
Browser DevTools (opened with F12 or Ctrl+Shift+I in Chrome/Edge, Cmd+Option+I on Mac) is the single most-used tool in a web QA engineer's day. It is a suite of panels built into the browser that let you inspect and manipulate the live page, watch network traffic, and read application state. Mastering it turns vague bug reports ('the button doesn't work') into precise, actionable findings ('clicking Submit sends a POST to /api/order that returns HTTP 500').

The Elements panel shows the live DOM and the CSS applied to any node. You click the inspect arrow, click a control, and immediately see its tag, attributes, and the cascade of style rules — including which rules were overridden. From here you can right-click an element and choose Copy > Copy selector or Copy XPath to get a starting locator, edit the HTML to test 'what if' scenarios, and toggle CSS states like :hover and :focus.

The Console panel is a live JavaScript prompt and the place where page errors surface. Red error messages (for example 'Uncaught TypeError' or a failed fetch) often explain why a feature is broken before you even look further. QA also uses the Console to validate locators with document.querySelector(...) and document.querySelectorAll(...).length, confirming a selector is unique and matches the intended element before committing it to a test.

The Network panel records every HTTP request the page makes: the URL, method (GET/POST), status code, response body, and timing. This is essential for bug investigation. A 404 means a resource is missing, a 401/403 means an auth problem, a 500 means a server error, and a slow request shows up as a long bar in the waterfall. You can filter by XHR/Fetch to see API calls, click a request to read its payload and response, and confirm whether a bug is front-end or back-end. Tip: enable 'Preserve log' so requests survive page navigation, and throttle the connection to reproduce slow-network bugs.

The Application panel exposes client-side storage and state: cookies, localStorage, sessionStorage, and service workers. QA uses it to check that an auth token or session cookie is set correctly, to clear storage and test a fresh-user experience, and to verify feature flags. Together these panels let one person reproduce, diagnose, and document a bug end to end without server access.
key_terms: Elements panel, Console, Network panel, Application panel, HTTP status code, XHR/Fetch, payload, waterfall, localStorage, preserve log
real_world_example: A user reports that saving their profile 'just spins forever'. You open DevTools, go to the Network panel, click Save, and see a POST to /api/profile that returns HTTP 500 with a response body of {"error":"email already in use"}. The Console also shows an unhandled promise rejection. Now your bug report is precise: the back end returns a 500 instead of a 400 validation message, and the front end never tells the user — you attach the request URL, status, and response payload as evidence.
step_by_step:
1. Press F12 (or Ctrl+Shift+I / Cmd+Option+I) to open DevTools.
2. In Elements, click the inspect arrow and click the control you're investigating; read its HTML and Styles.
3. Switch to Console and check for red errors; run document.querySelectorAll(yourSelector).length to validate a locator.
4. Open Network, check 'Preserve log', and reproduce the action.
5. Find the relevant request, read its Status, Headers, Payload, and Response tabs.
6. Interpret the status code (200 ok, 4xx client error, 5xx server error) and note timing.
7. In Application, inspect cookies/localStorage to confirm tokens or session state, clearing them to test fresh sessions.
8. Capture the URL, status, payload, and a screenshot to attach to the bug report.
common_mistakes:
Not enabling 'Preserve log', so the network requests vanish on page navigation and the failing call is lost.
Reading only the Elements panel and missing the obvious Console error or failed Network request that explains the bug.
Confusing HTTP status codes — for example calling a 401 (unauthorized) a 'server error' when 5xx codes are server errors.
Copying DevTools' auto-generated 'Copy selector' output and trusting it blindly; it is often long, brittle, and nth-child based.
Forgetting to clear cache/storage or disable cache when testing, so old assets mask whether a fix actually works.
practical_use_case: DevTools is used constantly: inspecting elements to build locators, validating selectors in the Console, watching the Network panel to determine if a defect is front-end or back-end, reading status codes and payloads for bug reports, and checking Application storage to verify auth/session behavior and reproduce fresh-user scenarios.
summary: Browser DevTools combines Elements (DOM and CSS), Console (errors and locator validation), Network (HTTP requests, status codes, payloads), and Application (cookies and storage) into one investigative toolkit. A QA engineer uses it to reproduce, diagnose, and document bugs precisely, turning 'it doesn't work' into a concrete, evidence-backed report.
example:
```text
Bug report assembled from DevTools evidence

Title: Saving profile returns HTTP 500 with no user feedback
Steps to reproduce:
  1. Log in, go to Profile, change email to an existing address, click Save.
Expected: Inline validation message 'Email already in use' (HTTP 400).
Actual: Button spins indefinitely; no message shown.
DevTools evidence (Network panel):
  Request:  POST https://app.example.com/api/profile
  Status:   500 Internal Server Error
  Payload:  {"email":"taken@example.com"}
  Response: {"error":"email already in use"}
Console: Uncaught (in promise) Error: Request failed with status 500
Environment: Chrome 124, staging, user id 8841
```
This report is actionable because it pins down the exact request, the wrong status code (500 instead of 400), and the missing front-end handling.
interactive: Open DevTools on any website, go to the Network panel, reload the page, and find one request. Report its URL, HTTP method, status code, and what the status code means. Then explain what 'Preserve log' would change if you navigated to another page.
expected_answer: A correct answer names a real request (for example GET https://site.com/api/config), its method (GET), and its status code with meaning — e.g. 200 OK means success, 304 Not Modified means served from cache, 404 means the resource was not found, 500 means a server error. 'Preserve log' keeps recorded requests in the Network panel across page navigations instead of clearing them on each load, which is essential when a bug causes a redirect or reload and you'd otherwise lose the failing request.
homework: Find a web app where some action triggers an API call (login, search, add-to-cart, or submit a form). Using the Network panel, document the request URL, method, status code, request payload, and response body for one successful call and, if you can produce one, one failing call (for example a wrong password). Note which panel told you whether the bug was front-end or back-end.
expected_result: A submission showing real captured requests with URL, method, status code, payload, and response for at least one call, correct interpretation of the status codes, and reasoning about front-end vs back-end (for example: the API returned a correct 401, but the UI showed a generic error, so the defect is in front-end error handling).

quiz.question: Which DevTools panel do you use to see the HTTP status code and response body of an API call?
An API request returns HTTP 500. What does that indicate?
Why enable 'Preserve log' in the Network panel?
answers: To keep recorded requests across page navigations/reloads

quiz.question: Where would you check whether a session/auth cookie was set correctly?

title: XPath selectors
short_description: Writing XPath expressions to locate elements by path, attributes, text, and relationships when CSS selectors aren't enough.
learning_goals:
Write absolute and (preferably) relative XPath expressions to locate any element.
Filter elements by attribute and by visible text using predicates and contains()/normalize-space().
Navigate the DOM with XPath axes such as parent, ancestor, following-sibling, and child.
Know when XPath is the right tool over CSS (text matching, traversing up to a parent) and how to keep expressions stable.
theory:
XPath (XML Path Language) is a query language for navigating the tree structure of an XML or HTML document. In automation it is one of the two main locator strategies (the other being CSS). XPath's superpower is that it can do things CSS cannot: select an element by its visible text, and traverse upward from a known element to a parent or ancestor. Because of this, QA reaches for XPath when a target has no good id/class but does have stable text, or when it must be found relative to a labeled neighbor.

There are two flavors. An absolute XPath starts from the root with a single slash, like /html/body/div[2]/form/button — it spells out the whole path and is extremely brittle because any structural change breaks it. A relative XPath starts with a double slash, like //button[@id='login'], meaning 'find a button with id login anywhere in the document.' Always prefer relative XPath; absolute paths are a beginner trap.

Predicates, written in square brackets, filter the matched set. //input[@name='email'] matches inputs whose name attribute equals email. //div[@class='card'][2] selects the second matching card (XPath indexes start at 1, not 0 — a common gotcha). You can combine conditions with and/or: //button[@type='submit' and @data-testid='save']. To match partial values use functions: //a[contains(@href,'/cart')] matches any anchor whose href contains /cart.

Text matching is XPath's signature feature. //button[text()='Log in'] matches a button whose exact text is 'Log in'. Because real text often has surrounding whitespace, use //button[normalize-space()='Log in'] to trim it, and //button[contains(text(),'Log')] for a partial match. This makes XPath ideal for locating controls that are only reliably identified by their human-readable label.

Axes let you move through relationships. //label[text()='Email']/following-sibling::input finds the input that follows the Email label. //td[text()='Order #1234']/parent::tr selects the whole table row containing that cell, so you can then find the Delete button within that row. Other useful axes are ancestor::, descendant::, preceding-sibling::, and child::. The trade-off: XPath is more powerful and more verbose than CSS, and in some browsers slightly slower, so use it where it adds value and prefer CSS or id/data-testid for the simple cases.
key_terms: absolute XPath, relative XPath, predicate, axis, contains(), normalize-space(), text(), following-sibling, parent, 1-based index
real_world_example: In an orders table you need to click the Delete button for the row whose order number is #1234, but every Delete button has the identical class 'btn-delete' and no unique id. You anchor on the unique text and traverse to the row, then back down to the button: //td[normalize-space()='#1234']/ancestor::tr//button[@aria-label='Delete']. This locates the correct Delete button by relationship to the known order number, something a plain CSS selector cannot do because CSS cannot select by text or walk up to an ancestor.
step_by_step:
1. Inspect the target element in DevTools and note its tag, attributes, and visible text.
2. Start relative with // and the tag, e.g. //button.
3. Add a predicate for the most stable attribute: //button[@data-testid='save'].
4. If no good attribute exists, match text: //button[normalize-space()='Save'].
5. If the target lacks identity, anchor on a unique neighbor (a label or a cell) and use an axis like following-sibling or ancestor to reach it.
6. Use contains() for partial/dynamic values: //div[contains(@class,'alert')].
7. Test it in the DevTools Console with $x("your xpath") and confirm it returns exactly one node.
common_mistakes:
Using absolute XPath like /html/body/div[3]/... which breaks the moment the page structure shifts.
Forgetting XPath indexes start at 1, not 0, so [0] never matches and [1] is the first element.
Matching exact text() when the element has leading/trailing whitespace; normalize-space() is needed.
Writing overly long, position-based chains instead of anchoring on a stable attribute or unique text.
Using single vs double quotes incorrectly inside the expression, causing syntax errors when the attribute value itself contains a quote.
practical_use_case: QA uses XPath when CSS can't do the job: selecting by visible label text, finding a row by its content and then acting on a button inside it, traversing up to a parent container, or pulling a value from a sibling cell. It is common in data-table tests, form-label-to-input relationships, and validating messages by their text.
summary: XPath is a tree-navigation language whose strengths are matching by visible text and traversing relationships, including upward to ancestors. Prefer relative XPath with stable attributes or unique text, use predicates and contains()/normalize-space() to filter, and remember indexes are 1-based.
example:
```python
# Selenium examples — locating elements with XPath
from selenium.webdriver.common.by import By

# 1. By a stable attribute (preferred)
driver.find_element(By.XPATH, "//button[@data-testid='login-submit']")

# 2. By exact visible text, whitespace-safe
driver.find_element(By.XPATH, "//button[normalize-space()='Log in']")

# 3. Partial attribute match
driver.find_element(By.XPATH, "//a[contains(@href, '/cart')]")

# 4. Find the Delete button in the row whose order number is #1234
driver.find_element(
    By.XPATH,
    "//td[normalize-space()='#1234']/ancestor::tr//button[@aria-label='Delete']"
)
```
Expression 4 anchors on the unique order-number cell, walks up to its <tr> row with ancestor::tr, then back down to the Delete button — a relationship-based locate that CSS cannot express.
interactive: Write a relative XPath that selects the <input> element that comes immediately after a <label> whose text is exactly 'Phone number'. Assume the markup is: <label>Phone number</label><input type='tel' name='phone'>.
expected_answer: A correct answer is //label[normalize-space()='Phone number']/following-sibling::input or, more specifically, //label[normalize-space()='Phone number']/following-sibling::input[1]. The expression anchors on the label by its text, then uses the following-sibling axis to reach the input that follows it. Using normalize-space() guards against stray whitespace, and [1] ensures you get the first following input if several exist. Matching by name (//input[@name='phone']) is also acceptable if that attribute is stable, but the task specifically asked to locate it relative to the label.
homework: On a page that contains a data table (orders, users, or products), write five XPath expressions: (1) select a specific cell by its exact text, (2) select the whole row containing that cell using an ancestor axis, (3) select an action button inside that row, (4) select an element by a partial attribute value with contains(), and (5) select a form input relative to its label using following-sibling. Test each in the Console with $x(...) and record how many nodes each returns.
expected_result: Five working relative XPath expressions (no absolute /html/body paths), each verified in the Console to return the intended single node where appropriate, demonstrating correct use of text()/normalize-space(), the ancestor and following-sibling axes, and contains(). The submission should note any that matched zero or multiple nodes and how they were refined.

quiz.question: Which XPath capability does a standard CSS selector NOT have?
answers: Selecting an element by its visible text and traversing up to an ancestor

quiz.question: In XPath, what index selects the FIRST matching element?
Which expression matches an anchor whose href contains '/cart'?
Why prefer relative XPath (//button[...]) over absolute XPath (/html/body/div[2]/...)?
answers: Relative XPath is the only valid syntax
Absolute XPath breaks easily when page structure changes
Relative XPath runs only in Firefox

title: CSS selectors
short_description: Writing concise, stable CSS selectors using id, class, attributes, and combinators to locate elements in automated tests.
learning_goals:
Write CSS selectors using tag, id (#), class (.), and attribute ([attr='value']) syntax.
Combine selectors with descendant, child (>), adjacent (+), and general sibling (~) combinators.
Use attribute-substring matchers (^=, $=, *=) and pseudo-classes like :nth-child() to target dynamic content.
Choose stable, readable CSS selectors and know the cases where CSS is preferable to XPath.
theory:
A CSS selector is a pattern that matches one or more elements in the DOM. It is the same syntax CSS uses for styling, reused by automation frameworks (Selenium's By.cssSelector, Playwright's locator, Cypress's get) to find elements. CSS selectors are usually shorter, faster, and more readable than XPath, so they are the default choice for the majority of locators; you only fall back to XPath for things CSS can't do (notably matching by visible text or traversing up to a parent).

The building blocks are simple. button matches by tag; #login matches the element with id="login"; .btn matches every element with class btn. You combine them: button.btn--primary matches buttons that also have the btn--primary class, and input#email targets an input with that id. Attribute selectors are especially valuable for QA: [data-testid='login-submit'] matches by a custom test attribute, [name='email'] by name, and [type='submit'] by type. These keep locators independent of fragile styling classes.

Combinators express relationships. A space is the descendant combinator: .modal button matches any button anywhere inside an element with class modal. The > is the direct-child combinator: ul > li matches only li elements that are immediate children of a ul. The + is the adjacent-sibling combinator: label + input matches the input immediately following a label. The ~ is the general-sibling combinator, matching any following sibling. Knowing these lets you scope a locator precisely so it doesn't accidentally match similar elements elsewhere on the page.

Substring attribute matchers handle dynamic values. [class^='btn'] matches classes starting with 'btn', [href$='.pdf'] matches links ending in .pdf, and [id*='user-'] matches any id containing 'user-'. These are useful when part of an attribute is stable and part is generated. Pseudo-classes add positional and state matching: li:nth-child(2) selects the second list item, tr:first-child and tr:last-child select boundary rows, and :not(.disabled) excludes elements. Note CSS :nth-child is 1-based, like the visual position.

The guiding principle is stability and readability. Prefer a single stable hook — [data-testid='...'] or #id — over long chains. When you must use structure, scope it minimally (for example .order-row[data-id='1234'] .delete instead of a deep nth-child path). Avoid selectors built purely from auto-generated class names or brittle positional indexes, because they break on the next release. A good CSS selector reads almost like a sentence and survives layout changes.
key_terms: tag selector, id selector (#), class selector (.), attribute selector, descendant combinator, child combinator (>), adjacent sibling (+), nth-child, substring matchers (^= $= *=), :not()
real_world_example: A sign-up form has three buttons that all share class 'btn btn--primary': Submit, Cancel, and a newsletter Subscribe. A bare .btn--primary selector matches all three and your test clicks the wrong one. You ask for stable hooks and use [data-testid='signup-submit']; where that isn't available yet, you scope by container: form#signup [type='submit']. Both target exactly the intended button and remain correct even when the marketing team restyles the buttons.
step_by_step:
1. Inspect the target in DevTools and look first for an id or a data-testid attribute.
2. If present, use it directly: #login or [data-testid='login-submit'].
3. If not, combine tag and the most stable class or attribute: input[name='email'].
4. Scope with a parent when needed to avoid duplicates: form#signup [type='submit'].
5. Use a combinator for relationships: label[for='email'] + input, or .modal > .footer button.
6. For dynamic attributes use substring matchers: [id^='user-'] or [href$='.pdf'].
7. Validate in the Console with document.querySelectorAll('your-selector').length and confirm it equals 1.
common_mistakes:
Using a shared class selector (.btn--primary) that matches several elements and clicking the wrong one.
Building long, position-dependent chains like div:nth-child(3) > div:nth-child(2) > span that break on any layout change.
Relying on auto-generated class names (css-1q2w3e) instead of stable ids or data-testid attributes.
Expecting CSS to match by visible text — it cannot; that requires XPath or framework-specific text locators.
Forgetting :nth-child is 1-based and that it counts among ALL siblings, not just siblings of the same type (use :nth-of-type when you mean same-type).
practical_use_case: CSS selectors are the everyday default for locating elements in Selenium, Playwright, and Cypress tests: targeting form fields by name/type, buttons by data-testid, scoping within a modal or a specific table row, and excluding disabled items. QA also types them straight into the DevTools Console to verify uniqueness before adding them to a test.
summary: CSS selectors locate elements with tag, id, class, and attribute patterns combined by descendant, child, and sibling combinators, plus substring matchers and pseudo-classes for dynamic and positional cases. Prefer a single stable hook like #id or [data-testid], scope minimally, validate uniqueness in the Console, and fall back to XPath only for text matching or upward traversal.
example:
```javascript
// Validate each selector in the DevTools Console: should return exactly 1
document.querySelectorAll("[data-testid='login-submit']").length;  // stable hook
document.querySelectorAll("input[name='email']").length;          // by name
document.querySelectorAll("form#signup [type='submit']").length;  // scoped
document.querySelectorAll("label[for='email'] + input").length;   // adjacent sibling
document.querySelectorAll(".order-row[data-id='1234'] .delete").length; // scoped row action
```
```python
# The same selectors used in Selenium
from selenium.webdriver.common.by import By

driver.find_element(By.CSS_SELECTOR, "[data-testid='login-submit']")
driver.find_element(By.CSS_SELECTOR, "input[name='email']")
driver.find_element(By.CSS_SELECTOR, ".order-row[data-id='1234'] .delete")
```
Each selector targets one element via a stable attribute or a scoped relationship rather than a fragile generated class or deep positional chain.
interactive: Three buttons on a page all have class 'btn btn--primary', but only one has the attribute data-action='submit'. Write a CSS selector that targets only the submit button, then write a second selector that targets the second <li> inside <ul class='menu'>.
expected_answer: For the submit button: .btn--primary[data-action='submit'] (or simply [data-action='submit'] if that attribute is unique). It combines the class with the distinguishing attribute so only the intended button matches. For the menu item: ul.menu li:nth-child(2) (or ul.menu > li:nth-child(2) to require a direct child). Remember :nth-child is 1-based, so :nth-child(2) is the second item; if the li elements are mixed with other sibling types, li:nth-of-type(2) would be safer.
homework: On a page with a form and at least one list or table, write six CSS selectors: (1) an input by name, (2) a button by a data-* attribute, (3) an element scoped inside a specific container, (4) a selector using a child or adjacent-sibling combinator, (5) one using a substring matcher (^=, $=, or *=), and (6) one using :nth-child. Validate each in the Console with querySelectorAll(...).length and record the count.
expected_result: Six valid CSS selectors, each verified in the Console to return the intended count (typically 1 for unique targets), demonstrating attribute selectors, container scoping, a combinator, a substring matcher, and a positional pseudo-class. The submission should prefer stable hooks (id/data-*) over generated classes and note any selector that matched the wrong number of elements and how it was tightened.

quiz.question: Which CSS selector matches an element with id="login"?
What does the combinator in 'ul > li' mean?
answers: Any li anywhere inside a ul
Only li elements that are direct children of a ul
The li immediately after a ul

quiz.question: Which selector matches links whose href ends with '.pdf'?
Which task can a CSS selector NOT do (requiring XPath instead)?
answers: Match an element by its exact visible text and traverse up to a parent
